title: Quiz: Quantum Classification Algorithms
description: Test your understanding of quantum machine learning approaches to classification

questions.question: What is the core idea behind quantum kernel methods?

options.text: Train quantum computers to memorize data
Map classical data to quantum states and compute their overlap as a kernel
Replace all classical computations with quantum ones
Use quantum entanglement to store data

questions.explanation: Quantum kernel methods map classical data to quantum states |φ(x)⟩ using a feature map circuit, then compute the kernel as the overlap |⟨φ(x)|φ(x')⟩|². This can access exponentially large feature spaces.

questions.question: What makes Variational Quantum Classifiers (VQC) suitable for NISQ devices?

options.text: They don't require any quantum operations
They use shallow parameterized circuits that can tolerate noise
They only work with perfect qubits
They use error-corrected quantum computers

questions.explanation: VQCs use shallow parameterized circuits that can be executed on noisy near-term devices. The parameters are optimized classically, making them hybrid quantum-classical algorithms that are more resilient to noise.

questions.question: In a QSVM, what computational task is offloaded to the quantum computer?

options.text: The entire SVM optimization problem
Only the final prediction
Computing the kernel matrix entries
Storing the support vectors

questions.explanation: In QSVM, the quantum computer computes the kernel matrix entries K(x,x') = |⟨φ(x)|φ(x')⟩|². The actual SVM optimization (finding support vectors and weights) is still done classically.

questions.question: What is the potential advantage of quantum feature maps?

options.text: They always produce better results than classical methods
They can access exponentially large feature spaces with polynomial resources
They eliminate the need for training data
They make all problems linearly separable

questions.explanation: With n qubits, quantum feature maps can access a 2^n dimensional feature space while only requiring polynomial resources. This can potentially capture complex patterns that are hard to represent classically.

questions.question: Quantum classifiers have already demonstrated practical advantage over classical methods for real-world credit scoring tasks.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: False. While quantum classifiers show theoretical promise, practical quantum advantage for credit scoring has not yet been demonstrated. Current hardware limitations and the need for specific problem structures make this an active research area.

questions.question: How does a VQC make predictions?

options.text: By measuring the quantum state of all qubits
By measuring expectation values or probabilities from the output state
By reading classical memory
By counting quantum errors

questions.explanation: VQCs encode input data into a quantum state, apply parameterized circuits, then extract predictions by measuring expectation values of observables or probabilities of specific measurement outcomes.

questions.question: What is the 'data loading bottleneck' in quantum machine learning?

options.text: Quantum computers can't store any data
Loading classical data into quantum states may require exponential resources
Quantum memory is too expensive
Data must be converted to binary

questions.explanation: The data loading bottleneck refers to the fact that encoding n classical data points into a quantum state (amplitude encoding) can require O(n) or more quantum operations, potentially eliminating any computational speedup.

questions.question: Which are valid quantum classification paradigms? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Quantum kernel methods (QSVM)
Variational quantum classifiers
Amplitude encoding classifiers
Classical logistic regression

questions.explanation: Quantum kernel methods, variational quantum classifiers, and amplitude encoding classifiers are all valid quantum classification paradigms. Classical logistic regression, while effective, is not a quantum approach.
